goal.p1: This project was born out of the desire to determine the real raise %, in relation to the loss of salary due to inflation since the previous raise.
goal.p2: If you want to delve deeper into the subject of inflation, there are many more detailed and serious sources.
goal.p3:
• Eurostat website 🇪🇺
  • Eurostat explanation of HICP
  • (🇫🇷) France's economic website
goal.title: Goal

legal.p1: No data is collected at any point.
legal.p2: This website is hosted by OVH, 2 rue Kellermann - 59100 Roubaix - France.
legal.title: Legal Notices

why.p1: In the course of 2022, inflation rates soared in France ( and in Europe overall ). Many wondered whether the annual "raises" would, at least, cover the rise in prices.
why.p2: Turns out the question was relevant for all previous years, but we don't often think about it.
why.title: About

custom.error: ⚠ Warning, pending modification not saved as the json data is not yet valid
custom.helper: Text must be a valid json structure listing only numbers with keys representing the date with format YYYY-MM
custom.label: Use custom inflation rates ✍️

imposition.label: Imposition (%)
imposition.helper: Percent withdrawn from the gross income to get the net salary

grossAnnual.helper: Imposition is withdrawn from the gross income, which is then evaluated monthly against the inflation rate
grossAnnual.label: Annuel gross salary

incomeMode.label: Calculation mode according the type of salary

netMonthly.helper: Monthly income is directly evaluated against the inflation rate
netMonthly.label: Monthly net salary

configure.saved: Parameters have been saved | Parameters have been saved ({count})

inflation.p1: Inflation rates used are thoses published by the EU commission Eurostat 🇪🇺 for each month (harmonised index of consumer prices, monthly rate of change).
inflation.title: Where do inflation rates come from?

operating.p1: The oldest salary is your reference income. This salary allows you to by a pile of stuff.
operating.p2: Over time, because of the inflation going on, the same pile of stuff is going to be increasingly expensive. As a result, you salary will allow you to buy only a smaller portion of that same pile of stuff.
operating.p3: Following raises are then recalculated to reflect how they allow you to buy the same pile of stuff.
operating.p4: The change in the ability to buy the pile of stuff is represented on the chart by the gap between the announced salary and the adjusted salary.
operating.title: How does it work?

p1.3: , fill in the successive salary changes over time.

usage.p2: You will be able to see the raise % announced, and below, the real change adjusted with the inflation.

p3.3: , you will be able to change the income calculation mode between annual gross income with imposition, and net monthly.

usage.title: How to use this tool?

date.label: Date of the change in salary

new.error: ⚠️ salary for this date {date} already filled in, remove the existing first
new.title: Add a salary

multi.adjusted: Including inflation, income didn't change. | In reality, income lost {changeAdjusted}% of its value. | In reality, income increased by {changeAdjusted}% of its value.
multi.announced: Over {period}, your salary has changed {numberOfChanges} times, without change in valuation. | Over {period}, your salary changed {numberOfChanges} times, with a announced loss of {change}%. | Over {period}, your salary changed {numberOfChanges} times, for a announced raise of {change}%.
multi.overall: From {baseIncome}{currency} to {finalIncome} {finalIncomeAdjusted}{currency} net/month over {period} months.

change.none: To start, add a salary to see it's evolution over time.
change.one: In {period}, you salary valuation did'nt change. | In {period}, your salary lost {changeAdjusted}% of its value. | In {period}, you salary valuation increased by {changeAdjusted}%.
change.helper: Add another salary to see the gap between them adjusted with the inflation.
